conceptSource: concepts1 — random across categories A–M (first batch using concepts1; 10 of 13 categories selected)

items.title: Hospital VIP Room Removes Old Woman → She Funded the Pediatric Floor

beats.setup: Private-wing administrator guides elderly woman out of VIP waiting area loudly saying 'this floor is for families of admitted patients only.'
beats.clue: Pediatric attending rounds the corner, sees the woman, drops his tablet mid-stride.
beats.turn: Attending takes administrator's hand off woman's elbow, says her name — 'Mrs. Aldridge' — in the voice you use when you owe someone your department.
beats.payoff: Eleanor Aldridge donated $14M anonymously in 2019 to build the pediatric cardiac floor; grandson is admitted three rooms away; attending reveals she funded his fellowship; administrator reassigned by end of shift.

items.peakTensionMoment: Administrator gripping Eleanor's elbow mid-escort toward elevator; Eleanor in worn beige cardigan not resisting; attending frozen mid-stride with tablet dangling; donor wall of brass plaques visible behind them.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Eleanor Aldridge — 81, silver hair in a loose bun, worn beige cardigan over floral blouse, simple flat shoes, thin gold wedding band only, quiet dignified posture
• Craig Pallister (administrator) — 39, navy hospital scrubs, lanyard with photo ID, hand on Eleanor's elbow, impatient tight mouth
• Dr. Samuel Osei (pediatric attending) — 44, white coat over blue scrubs, stethoscope at neck, tablet in left hand, frozen mid-stride
• Setting — Maine Medical Center private wing lobby, Portland ME, Wednesday 2pm, soft winter light through frosted glass partitions, donor wall of brass plaques, elevator bay
• Object clue — donor wall brass plaque 'ANONYMOUS 2019' (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
items.photoPrompt:
4:5 vertical photo, smartphone snapshot aesthetic, natural unposed moment, realistic phone-camera detail.

Maine Medical Center private wing lobby, Portland, Maine, Wednesday afternoon around 2pm. Soft winter light diffuses through frosted glass partitions along the corridor; polished linoleum floor reflects recessed ceiling lamps; a wall of small brass donor plaques runs the length of the hall beside the elevator bay. Craig Pallister (administrator), 39, navy hospital scrubs with a photo-ID lanyard, impatient tight mouth, right hand gripping Eleanor's left elbow mid-escort; Eleanor Aldridge, 81, silver hair in a loose bun, worn beige cardigan over a floral blouse, simple flat shoes, thin gold wedding band on her left hand only, quiet dignified posture, not resisting; Dr. Samuel Osei (pediatric attending), 44, white coat over blue scrubs, stethoscope draped at his neck, tablet dangling from his left hand, frozen mid-stride three steps behind the pair.

The photo captures the second the attending freezes: Craig has Eleanor by the elbow and is guiding her toward the elevator bay at a pace faster than her flat shoes want to go, his mouth half-open on whatever phrase he uses to move people along. Eleanor's free hand rests against the strap of a worn canvas tote at her side; her eyes are forward, patient, not fighting. Three steps behind them, Dr. Osei has stopped walking — his weight caught on his back foot, tablet tipping from his left hand, face mid-recognition. Behind the three figures, the brass donor-plaque wall is in soft focus, dozens of small brass rectangles catching the recessed ceiling light.

Framing: handheld eye-level POV from a nurse near the reception desk, slightly off-center so Craig's grip on Eleanor's elbow and Dr. Osei's frozen stride share the central vertical with the donor wall visible behind all three. Shallow phone depth-of-field — Craig's hand, Eleanor's cardigan, and Dr. Osei's dropped tablet sharp; the donor plaques, the elevator bay, and the frosted glass partitions softly blurred. Candid, like a floor nurse who saw something wrong and raised her phone. No readable text on the donor plaques, Craig's photo-ID lanyard, hospital signage, Dr. Osei's tablet screen, or Eleanor's tote bag label — keep all printed and engraved surfaces angled away or out of focus. No subtitles. No watermark.

items.title: Driver Forced to Take Blame → Pulls Out Recorder

beats.setup: CFO's adult son crashed a company Mercedes into a garage pillar after drinks; the family's longtime driver Ray is being pressured to sign a statement taking the blame 'for insurance purposes.'
beats.clue: Ray reaches into inner pocket and pulls out a small digital voice recorder; the red LED is already blinking.
beats.turn: Ray presses play. The garage speakers carry the CFO's own voice: 'Ray, take him home. If anything happens, you were driving. His father asked me to protect him.'
beats.payoff: The 'father' is the company's deceased founder who gave Ray the recorder years ago; HR witnesses reverse testimony on the spot; CFO's son charged with DUI; Ray declines severance because the founder's will already named him as a trust beneficiary.

items.peakTensionMoment: CFO in charcoal overcoat holding typed statement toward Ray; Ray in black driver's jacket beside crumpled Mercedes with one hand in inner pocket, red LED visible; two HR witnesses with clipboards three steps back; fluorescent garage light on concrete and broken glass.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Ray Watkins — 62, Black, salt-pepper close-crop hair, black driver's jacket with brass pin at lapel, calm steady hands, one hand in inner pocket where recorder LED glows
• Victor Caldwell (CFO) — 54, charcoal overcoat over loosened tie, ruddy face, typed statement held toward Ray
• Setting — Whitmore Capital corporate parking garage level B2, Memphis TN, Friday 6pm, cold fluorescent underground light, concrete pillars, crumpled silver Mercedes against pillar, broken glass
• Object clue — small digital voice recorder with red LED in Ray's inner jacket pocket (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
items.photoPrompt:
4:5 vertical photo, smartphone snapshot aesthetic, natural unposed moment, realistic phone-camera detail.

Whitmore Capital corporate parking garage level B2, Memphis, Tennessee, Friday evening around 6pm. Cold fluorescent tubes cast flat white light on painted concrete walls; a crumpled silver Mercedes sedan sits nosed into a concrete pillar, left headlight shattered, glass scattered in a semicircle; tire-mark arc leads into the pillar. Victor Caldwell (CFO), 54, charcoal overcoat over a loosened tie, ruddy face, typed statement and pen held at arm's length toward another man; Ray Watkins, 62, Black, salt-pepper close-crop hair, black driver's jacket with a small brass pin at the lapel, calm posture beside the crumpled Mercedes, right hand inside his inner jacket pocket where a faint red LED glow is just visible through the fabric; two HR witnesses in business attire three steps back with clipboards.

The photo captures the moment the object clue appears: Victor's right hand extends the typed statement toward Ray at arm's length, jaw set. Ray's right hand is inside his inner jacket pocket — calm, deliberate — and a faint red dot glows through the black fabric. Ray's expression is steady, unhurried. The two HR witnesses have their clipboards raised but pens are not moving. Broken glass from the Mercedes headlight glints under the fluorescent tubes.

Framing: handheld low-angle POV from a garage attendant six paces away, slightly off-center so the typed statement and the red LED glow share the middle third with the crumpled Mercedes behind. Shallow phone depth-of-field — Victor's outstretched hand, Ray's jacket pocket with LED glow, and the broken glass sharp; the concrete depth, HR witnesses, and fluorescent tubes softly blurred. Candid, like a garage attendant raised his phone when the voices started. No readable text on the typed statement, Victor's company badge, Ray's brass pin, the Mercedes plate, HR clipboards, or garage signage — keep all printed surfaces angled away or out of focus. No subtitles. No watermark.

items.title: New Chief Restructures Dispatch → Old Dispatcher Plays 18-Year-Old 911 Recording

beats.setup: New Police Chief Marcus Devane announces dispatch-division restructuring at all-hands; the last name on the list is Gloria Raines, 63, thirty-one years at the desk.
beats.clue: Gloria reaches under her console and pulls out a small portable cassette player from behind a taped photograph; overhead PA crackles.
beats.turn: Gloria presses play. A panicked child's voice from eighteen years ago — calling 911, giving a street address. The voice is unmistakably the same voice that just read the restructuring list.
beats.payoff: The 911 call is from six-year-old Marcus Devane in 1997; Gloria stayed on the line for forty-one minutes, vectored patrol to his location; Marcus's mother did not survive; Marcus survived because Gloria held the line; restructuring list withdrawn; Gloria retires on her own terms six months later with a commendation signed by Marcus.

items.peakTensionMoment: Chief Devane at podium with printed list; Gloria at console with headset around neck, right hand on cassette player pulled from behind a taped photograph; dispatch screens glowing blue; thirty dispatchers turning to look.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Gloria Raines — 63, Black, silver-streaked braids pulled back, navy dispatch uniform polo, headset around neck, thirty-one-year service pin on collar, right hand on small portable cassette player
• Chief Marcus Devane — 42, dress uniform with badge and tie bar, close-cropped black hair, printed list in right hand
• Setting — Albuquerque PD dispatch center, NM, Monday 9am, overhead fluorescent, wall of blue dispatch screens, rolling desk chairs, podium at front
• Object clue — small portable cassette player from behind taped photograph at Gloria's console (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
items.photoPrompt:
4:5 vertical photo, smartphone snapshot aesthetic, natural unposed moment, realistic phone-camera detail.

Albuquerque Police Department dispatch center, Albuquerque, New Mexico, Monday morning around 9am. Overhead fluorescent panels throw flat light across rows of dispatch consoles with blue-glowing multi-monitor arrays; rolling desk chairs in navy fabric; small podium with microphone at the front near a whiteboard. Chief Marcus Devane, 42, dress uniform with badge and tie bar, close-cropped black hair, at the podium with printed list in right hand, mouth open mid-sentence; Gloria Raines, 63, Black, silver-streaked braids pulled back, navy dispatch uniform polo, headset around her neck, thirty-one-year service pin on collar, seated at her console in the second row with right hand on a small portable cassette player pulled from behind a taped photograph at her station; thirty other dispatchers in rolling chairs turning their heads from the podium toward Gloria.

The photo captures the second the room goes wrong: Chief Devane is still speaking, printed list in hand, unaware that thirty heads have turned away from him. Gloria's right hand rests on the small silver-gray cassette player on her console desk; her left hand still touches the edge of a taped photograph. Her expression is calm and certain. The thirty dispatchers have half-turned — some toward Gloria, some toward the PA speaker above, all no longer looking at the chief.

Framing: handheld over-the-shoulder POV from a dispatcher at the back row, slightly low so the chief at the podium fills the upper-left third and Gloria with the cassette player fills the lower-right third. Shallow phone depth-of-field — Chief's printed list, Gloria's hand on cassette player, and the taped photograph edge sharp; dispatch screens, whiteboard, fluorescent panels softly blurred. Candid, like a dispatcher quietly raised their phone the second the room's energy changed. No readable text on the printed list, taped photograph, dispatch screens, console labels, whiteboard, Gloria's service pin, or badge — keep all text angled away or out of focus. No subtitles. No watermark.

items.title: Funeral Director Impatient with Homeless Man → Man Places Folded Letter on Casket

beats.setup: Funeral director tries to quietly remove a homeless man from a private viewing; the man does not resist but does not leave; family in front row watches silently.
beats.clue: Man reaches into his coat and places a folded yellowed letter in blue-ink handwriting on the closed casket; the attorney's widow sees the handwriting and her hand goes to her throat.
beats.turn: Widow stands, walks past funeral director, picks up the letter, reads the first line, sits down as if her legs have stopped working.
beats.payoff: Letter is in the deceased attorney's handwriting, addressed 'Dear Thomas' — acknowledges Thomas as his older brother estranged since 1981; attorney had been searching for Thomas for fifteen years; Thomas received the letter two days after the attorney died; funeral director dismissed; widow invites Thomas to sit in the front pew.

items.peakTensionMoment: Funeral director with hand on Thomas's shoulder guiding toward door; Thomas standing at casket end with yellowed letter held in both hands; widow in black dress in front pew turning, hand at her throat; dim amber sconce light, white lilies on casket.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Thomas Crane — 68, weathered face, gray beard, layered brown field coat, worn boots, yellowed folded letter in both hands, quiet eyes
• Gerald Fisk (funeral director) — 55, black suit, white gloves, hand on Thomas's shoulder
• Margaret Crane-Whitfield (widow) — 64, black dress, pearl clip earrings, hand at throat, eyes wide
• Setting — Greenwood Chapel funeral home, Louisville KY, Thursday 10am, dim amber sconce light, mahogany pews, closed casket at altar with white lilies, stained-glass window behind
• Object clue — yellowed folded letter in blue-ink handwriting addressed 'Dear Thomas' (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
items.photoPrompt:
4:5 vertical photo, smartphone snapshot aesthetic, natural unposed moment, realistic phone-camera detail.

Greenwood Chapel funeral home viewing room, Louisville, Kentucky, Thursday morning around 10am. Dim amber sconce light from brass wall fixtures casts warm pools on dark mahogany pews; closed mahogany casket on a raised platform at front with white lilies at both ends; stained-glass window behind throws muted color into the amber. Thomas Crane, 68, weathered face, gray beard, layered brown field coat over faded flannel, worn boots, yellowed folded letter held carefully in both hands at chest height, quiet eyes focused on the casket; Gerald Fisk (funeral director), 55, black suit, white gloves, right hand on Thomas's left shoulder from behind; Margaret Crane-Whitfield (widow), 64, black dress, pearl clip earrings, seated in front pew, body half-turned, left hand at her throat, eyes wide.

The photo captures the silent witness: Thomas has stopped at the casket end closest to the exit, funeral director's white-gloved hand still on his shoulder, but Thomas is not moving toward the door. He holds the folded yellowed letter at chest height in both hands — creases showing permanent lines. The funeral director's professional face is tight. In the front pew, the widow has turned; her hand is at her throat; she has not yet spoken. Six other family members sit behind her, most still facing forward, not yet aware. White lilies catch the amber sconce light.

Framing: handheld eye-level POV from a mourner in the third pew on the side aisle, slightly off-center so Thomas at the casket end and the widow in the front pew share the central diagonal with the funeral director's white glove between them. Shallow phone depth-of-field — Thomas's hands on the letter, the white glove, and the widow's eyes sharp; mahogany pews, stained-glass, other family softly blurred. Candid, like a mourner who sensed something wrong and quietly raised their phone. No readable text on the yellowed letter, memorial program, casket nameplate, wall plaques, or funeral-home signage — keep all handwriting and printed surfaces angled away or out of focus. No subtitles. No watermark.

items.title: Insurance Adjuster Rejects Transplant → Patient's Daughter: She Donated Her Kidney to Your CEO's Son

beats.setup: Insurance adjuster stamps DENIED on transplant-coverage request without looking up, slides folder back to a young woman who drove four hours; tells her the exclusion is 'standard.'
beats.clue: Priya Doss opens her phone, scrolls to a photograph, and sets it face-up on the desk: hospital photo of her mother shaking hands with a man in a visitor's badge — the man wears the insurance company's logo.
beats.turn: Adjuster looks at the photo. The man is the company's CEO. The woman is Priya's mother. The handshake is dated 2019.
beats.payoff: Priya's mother Sunita donated a kidney to the CEO's teenage son in 2019; CEO personally promised ongoing care coverage 'for anything your family ever needs'; Priya has his handwritten thank-you card; transplant approved within 72 hours; lifetime-care amendment issued; adjuster moved to non-patient-facing role.

items.peakTensionMoment: Kevin Marsh behind cubicle desk with DENIED stamp hovering; Priya across desk in worn denim jacket, calm defiant face, phone placed face-up showing hospital photo; fluorescent light, cubicle walls, manila folders.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Priya Doss — 24, dark hair in low ponytail, worn denim jacket over plain t-shirt, calm defiant dark eyes, phone placed on desk
• Kevin Marsh (adjuster) — 38, white dress shirt, loosened navy tie, DENIED stamp in right hand, glasses pushed up on forehead
• Setting — Great Plains Health insurance regional office, Omaha NE, Tuesday 3pm, sterile fluorescent open-plan cubicle office, partition walls, manila folders
• Object clue — phone showing hospital photo of mother shaking hands with CEO (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
items.photoPrompt:
4:5 vertical photo, smartphone snapshot aesthetic, natural unposed moment, realistic phone-camera detail.

Great Plains Health insurance regional office, Omaha, Nebraska, Tuesday afternoon around 3pm. Sterile fluorescent panels wash flat light across open-plan cubicle office; beige partition walls; stacks of manila folders on every surface. Kevin Marsh (adjuster), 38, white dress shirt with loosened navy tie, glasses pushed up on forehead, seated behind cubicle desk with red rubber DENIED stamp in right hand hovering above an open manila folder; Priya Doss, 24, dark hair in low ponytail, worn denim jacket over plain white t-shirt, calm defiant dark eyes, seated across desk with her phone placed face-up on the desk surface showing a photograph.

The photo captures the accuser's confidence cracking: Kevin's DENIED stamp is frozen mid-air above the folder — a red DENIED mark visible on the top page — but his eyes have just moved from the folder to the phone Priya has placed between them. The phone screen shows a hospital-room photograph of two figures shaking hands. Priya's calm dark eyes are locked on Kevin's face. Her posture is upright, not pleading. One hand rests flat beside the phone.

Framing: handheld eye-level POV from a coworker in the cubicle aisle behind Priya, slightly low so the phone and Kevin's frozen stamp share the frame center. Shallow phone depth-of-field — the DENIED stamp, phone screen, and Priya's face sharp; cubicle partitions, manila folders, fluorescent tubes softly blurred. Candid, like a coworker who heard the stamp hit and looked over the partition. No readable text on the stamp impression, folder contents, phone-screen badge, company-logo plaque, Kevin's ID badge, or desk paperwork — keep all text angled away or out of focus. No subtitles. No watermark.

items.title: Principal Cancels Janitor's Retirement → School Board Chair: He Paid for 14 Students

beats.setup: Principal announces janitor's retirement ceremony canceled due to 'budget constraints'; Mr. Okonkwo nods once and says nothing.
beats.clue: School board chair Dr. Leah Simmons walks into gym unannounced with manila folder, asks why gymnasium is empty.
beats.turn: Simmons opens folder: fourteen graduation-fee receipts paid in cash with Okonkwo's handwriting on the memo line, spanning twenty-two years.
beats.payoff: Okonkwo paid $180–$350 per student for those who couldn't afford graduation fees for 22 years out of his custodial salary; one of those students is Dr. Simmons herself; ceremony reinstated on the spot; 400 students file in; principal formally reprimanded; district names scholarship fund after Okonkwo.

items.peakTensionMoment: Okonkwo in gray coveralls with mop resting against folded chair, standing still in empty gymnasium; Principal Voss at gym door with clipboard, back turned; Dr. Simmons entering from far door with manila folder; rows of empty folding chairs.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Emmanuel Okonkwo — 67, Nigerian-American, gray custodial coveralls with name patch 'OKONKWO', close-cropped gray hair, reading glasses on cord, mop resting against folded chair
• Principal Diana Voss — 48, navy pantsuit, clipboard under arm, back turned
• Dr. Leah Simmons (school board chair) — 36, charcoal blazer, natural hair pulled back, manila folder in hand
• Setting — Saguaro Ridge High School gymnasium, Tucson AZ, Friday 3pm, harsh overhead gym floods, empty folding chairs on polished wood floor
• Object clue — manila folder with fourteen graduation-fee receipts in Okonkwo's handwriting (pays off in full story Act 3–4)

items.title: Judge Dismisses Motion → Court Reporter: That's the Same Argument That Overturned Martinez

beats.setup: Judge Briggs dismisses public defender's habeas motion at 4:50pm Friday without reading it — 'Denied. We're done.'
beats.clue: Court reporter Alicia Vang stops typing mid-proceeding for the first time in eleven years. She looks up.
beats.turn: Alicia says on the record: 'That is the same legal argument that overturned the Martinez conviction in this circuit last month. I transcribed both.'
beats.payoff: Judge Briggs cannot dismiss after it's identified on the record as identical to a ruling he publicly endorsed; motion reopened Monday; defendant released within 30 days; Alicia receives judicial commendation for 'extraordinary service to the integrity of the record.'

items.peakTensionMoment: Judge at bench with motion folder mid-toss; public defender Maya Chen frozen with hands flat on folder; court reporter Alicia Vang below bench with hands lifted off stenotype keys, head turned up, mouth open; wall clock 4:50; fluorescent light.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Alicia Vang — 34, Hmong-American, dark hair in neat low bun, court-reporter navy blazer, hands lifted off stenotype keys, head turned up
• Judge Harold Briggs — 61, black robes, reading glasses on nose, motion folder mid-toss, impatient face
• Maya Chen (public defender) — 29, charcoal suit, hands flat on motion folder at defense table
• Setting — Kanawha County Courthouse courtroom, Charleston WV, Friday 4:50pm, fluorescent overhead, elevated bench, defense table, court-reporter station, wall clock visible
• Object clue — motion folder mid-toss + court reporter's hands lifted off stenotype (pays off in full story Act 3–4)

items.title: Diner Owner Humiliates Elderly Customer → He Invented the Waffle Iron Your Menu Runs On

beats.setup: Diner owner scolds elderly man for taking too long to order during Saturday rush — 'if you can't decide in thirty seconds, you're holding up paying customers.'
beats.clue: Longtime regular at end of counter looks at framed patent drawing above coffee station — then at old man — then back at drawing.
beats.turn: Regular says loud enough for counter: 'Marcy, the man you just yelled at invented the waffle iron hanging on your wall. The patent made your grandfather's first location possible.'
beats.payoff: Harold Lund holds the 1971 patent for the continuous-feed waffle iron that became the industry standard; Marcy's grandfather licensed it in 1974; Harold was a regular at the original location until it closed in 2003; came to new location for first time today; Marcy apologizes publicly; lifetime tab given; Saturday special renamed 'Harold's Original.'

items.peakTensionMoment: Marcy behind chrome counter pointing at menu board mid-scold; Harold on red-vinyl stool in worn corduroy jacket and flat cap with reading glasses low on nose; regular half-turned with eyes moving between Harold and framed patent drawing above coffee station; morning sun catching chrome.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Harold Lund — 84, thin white hair under flat cap, worn tan corduroy jacket, reading glasses low on nose, patient gentle face
• Marcy Trask (diner owner) — 45, white server's apron over flannel shirt, dark hair in high ponytail, pointing at menu board, scowling
• Doug (regular) — 58, trucker cap, flannel, half-turned on stool, eyes wide
• Setting — Rosie's Original Waffle House, Boise ID, Saturday 8am, morning sun through plate-glass, chrome counter, red-vinyl stools, framed patent drawing above coffee station
• Object clue — framed 1971 patent drawing of continuous-feed waffle iron with signature in corner (pays off in full story Act 3–4)

items.title: Naturalization Ceremony Official Is Rude → Senator Stands: He Saved Eleven Americans

beats.setup: Federal naturalization officer conducts a perfunctory rude ceremony, rushing through names, mispronouncing several; stumbles over candidate #147 and moves on without correction.
beats.clue: A sitting US Senator in the guest section stands from her folding chair before being introduced; shakes off aide's hand on her sleeve.
beats.turn: Senator walks to podium, takes mic: 'I want to acknowledge this man specifically' — points at candidate #147.
beats.payoff: Candidate #147 is Karim Al-Rashid, former Iraqi interpreter who in 2004 guided eleven Americans including the senator's nephew out of a collapsing compound under fire in Fallujah; spent seventeen years in immigration pipeline; senator wrote recommendation in 2009; officer placed on administrative review; Karim receives certificate from senator's hands; standing ovation.

items.peakTensionMoment: Officer at podium with clipboard; Karim in pressed white shirt standing in third row with American flag lapel pin; Senator Holt rising from guest section in navy blazer, aide's hand on sleeve; 200 candidates in folding chairs, American flags flanking podium.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Karim Al-Rashid — 58, olive skin, close-cropped salt-pepper hair, pressed white shirt, dark slacks, small American flag lapel pin, steady calm eyes
• Officer Dale Henning — 52, navy federal uniform, clipboard, glasses low on nose, impatient posture
• Senator Patricia Holt — 61, navy blazer, silver hair in French twist, pearl studs, rising from folding chair
• Setting — John H. Wood Jr. Federal Building ceremony hall, San Antonio TX, Friday 10am, fluorescent overhead, American flags flanking podium, 200 candidates in folding chairs
• Object clue — small American flag lapel pin on Karim's shirt (pays off in full story Act 3–4)

items.title: Megachurch Leader Dismisses Small Congregation → Elderly Pastor: We Were Your First Donors

beats.setup: Megachurch executive pastor offers smooth forced apology to elderly pastor requesting a partnership grant: 'your congregation doesn't currently meet demonstrated growth metrics.'
beats.clue: Pastor Ezra Williams does not stand to leave. He reaches into his worn suit jacket and pulls out a rubber-banded bundle of cancelled checks — old, faded, from a bank that closed in 2001.
beats.turn: Ezra sets the bundle on the glass table: 'Darren, my congregation was the first to donate to yours. Before you had a building. I have the cancelled checks.'
beats.payoff: Grace Covenant received its founding $12,000 seed donation in 1994 from Williams Chapel AME (Ezra's 40-member congregation) when predecessor held services in a rented cafeteria; twelve monthly payments of $1,000 from a congregation that never exceeded forty members; Grace Covenant's current annual budget is $23M; partnership denial reversed within the hour; megachurch board votes $500K perpetual endowment to Williams Chapel; Darren apologizes to full Williams Chapel congregation the following Sunday.

items.peakTensionMoment: Darren in fitted charcoal suit at head of oak table, forced-apology smile; Pastor Ezra in worn navy suit with threadbare tie, bundle of faded checks held in both weathered hands; glass-walled room with empty 4,000-seat sanctuary through window; Bible on table between them.
items.characterBible:
CHARACTER LOCK — do not change across photo, caption, and full story:
• Pastor Ezra Williams — 79, Black, white close-cropped hair, worn navy suit with threadbare navy tie, wire-rim glasses, weathered hands holding rubber-banded bundle of faded cancelled checks
• Darren Cole (executive pastor) — 44, fitted charcoal suit, no tie, styled brown hair, forced-apology smile, fingertips together
• Setting — Grace Covenant Megachurch conference room, Little Rock AR, Tuesday 11am, glass-walled room overlooking empty 4,000-seat sanctuary, long polished oak table, single Bible on table
• Object clue — rubber-banded bundle of faded 1994 cancelled checks from Williams Chapel AME (pays off in full story Act 3–4)
